> @prajjwal-arista Do you see delay of more than 90sec for the bmc link to be up and configured IP address ? This is causing the race condition.
> 
> In the testbed I used to validate, I see there is a delay of 30sec before bmc0 interface is UP and IP is configured -- hence we don't hit this condition.
> 
> But if you see this race condition, this approach to separate the redis server start and bind redis server to the bmc interface/IP later is a good approach.
> 
> @chander-nexthop @oleksandrivantsiv @nats-nokia to review this PR as well

We do see a delay of more than 30s seconds. Also 30s won't be enough for the first boot because assignment of IP to the bmc0 interface is left to the vendor and on the first boot there would be additional delay due to generation of machine.conf and then installation of platform modules themselves.

This seems like hiding the root cause. Either we need break-system-packages rule or not, there shouldn't be best effort installation enforcement. Looks like we will often just ignore the policing added earlier for the rule.

> This seems like hiding the root cause. Either we need break-system-packages rule or not, there shouldn't be best effort installation enforcement. Looks like we will often just ignore the policing added earlier for the rule.

Hi @vaibhavhd, with the change in `scripts/docker_version_control.sh`, the build pulls `p4lang/behavioral-model` pinned by the version file `files/build/versions/default/versions-docker`(reproducible build). currently the pinned one (old version image) in the version file does not support `pip3 install --break-system-packages` (this is what we had before, working with no break-system-package rule `pip3 install supervisord-dependent-startup`)
But when we run the weekly upgrade version pipeline, the build will pull the latest `p4lang/behavioral-model`. It needs the `--break-system-packages` rule. 
We can remove the `|| pip3 install supervisord-dependent-startup` after the version files get updated. how does this sound to you? and you're right, we should've chosen a clearer way which is to update the pinned `p4lang/behavioral-model` to the newest in the version file and only keep the `--break-system-packages` rule instead of waiting for the version upgrade pipeline to update.

Hi @bhouse-nexthop — this builds on your SBOM work (#27455), so your review would be appreciated.

**`build_sbom.py`:** the per-container SBOM carried no OS/distro context, so grype silently skipped every deb/OS package (docker-sonic-mgmt only reported npm CVEs). This detects the container distro from `/etc/os-release`, emits an `operating-system` component with `syft:distro:*`, and adds a `distro=` qualifier to deb PURLs. The aggregate `.bin` path is unchanged.

**`docker-sonic-mgmt.yml`:** enables the `sbom_vuln_scan` job you'd gated with `condition: false` — flagging since it was deliberate; happy to drop that and land only the `build_sbom.py` fix if you'd prefer.

Verified on the real SBOM: grype went 23 (npm-only) → 114 (23 npm + 91 deb).

Thanks!

While auditing all `hash_iterate` call sites in this file, I noticed a similar pattern at another place:

https://github.com/sonic-net/sonic-buildimage/blob/d3a7f642187828cdfc454f4a6d5fe5b14835ad53/src/sonic-frr/dplane_fpm_sonic/dplane_fpm_sonic.c#L3294

Here `zl3vni` is passed as the callback argument, but [`fpm_unset_rmac_table()`](https://github.com/sonic-net/sonic-buildimage/blob/d3a7f642187828cdfc454f4a6d5fe5b14835ad53/src/sonic-frr/dplane_fpm_sonic/dplane_fpm_sonic.c#L3283-L3288) never uses it, so it doesn't cause any errors. However, passing `NULL` would better communicate intent.

The same pattern exists in upstream FRR at [`dplane_fpm_nl.c` L1502](https://github.com/FRRouting/frr/blob/15e3f2f71a1108b575ee12955c719e68675572d1/zebra/dplane_fpm_nl.c#L1502) and has been there since 2019. Not addressing that in this PR since it's purely cosmetic and has no functional impact.

| Build | kill -9 zebra @ RIB v4=6453 / v6=6529 | Result |
|---|---|---|

| **FIXED** (this PR's FRR delta, container-swap) | 3× identical full-RIB kills | **SURVIVED — 0 cores, 0 asserts** |

## CI failure is not caused by this PR

The failing check is a `sonic-utilities` unit test, `tests/sonic_package_manager/test_registry.py::test_get_registry_for`, which this FRR-patch backport does not touch. It is an environmental dependency regression currently hitting 202605 (and master) utilities PR-checkers generally.

**Root cause:** `setup.py` pins `docker-image-py>=0.1.10` with no upper bound, so CI floated up to the newly released **0.2.0**. 0.2.0 rewrote `split_docker_domain` to add a branch: *if the first path component is not all-lowercase, treat it as a registry domain* (Docker repository namespaces must be lowercase). The test fixture `get_registry_for('Azure/sonic')` has an uppercase `A`, so under 0.2.0 it resolves to a registry host `Azure` instead of Docker Hub, and `assert registry is resolver.DockerHubRegistry` fails. Under the previously-installed 0.1.13 the same string normalized to `docker.io`.

| Reference | 0.1.13 | 0.2.0 |
|---|---|---|
| `Azure/sonic` | Docker Hub | **host `Azure`** |
| `azure/sonic` | Docker Hub | Docker Hub |

**Potential fixes (being discussed internally):**
- Pin `docker-image-py` to `0.1.13` (immediate unblock).
- Lowercase the test fixture to `azure/sonic` — a legal Docker namespace that resolves to Docker Hub under **both** versions, removing the need to pin.
- Make the resolver's domain split independent of the library's normalization behavior.

### AI-assisted review

Nice migration overall — the SMBus ioctl path is ABI-correct and faithful to the old `i2cget/i2cset` semantics (and drops the per-call `sudo`+subprocess overhead), the sysfs conversion is clean, and the packaging wiring is complete: the existing `debian/rules` is already generic over `MODULE_DIRS := h6-128` and conditionally builds+installs `modules/*.ko`, so adding `modules/Makefile` is enough to get `cb_pld.ko` compiled and packaged (verified — no `debian/rules` change needed). A handful of grounded items to tidy before merge, mostly on the kernel side.

**cb_pld.c**

1. **I²C read errors get truncated into `u8` in every `show_*`.** `cpld_i2c_read()` returns `int` and can be negative on bus error, but the show functions do `u8 val = cpld_i2c_read(...)`, so a failure becomes a bogus positive byte emitted to userspace. Please propagate the error out of the show function (e.g. return `-EIO`) — at least for `misc`, since the Python `get_oper_status()` trusts that value.

2. **Non-atomic read-modify-write in `set_console_wdt`.** `cpld_i2c_read()` takes no lock while `cpld_i2c_write()` locks `update_lock` internally, so the read and the write straddle two separate critical sections and a concurrent writer can lose a bit-update. Suggest holding `update_lock` across the whole read→modify→write (a locked RMW helper).

3. **Unused locals → possible `-Werror` build break.** `set_misc` (`reg_val`, `mask`), and `set_mux_sel` / `set_reset_sig` / `set_bios_red` (`mask`) declare variables that are never used (only `set_console_wdt` uses them). Kernel-module builds often run with `-Werror`, so please remove them — or, if these setters were meant to be masked RMWs like `set_console_wdt`, implement that instead.

4. **`reset_cause` is dead code.** The probe init that reads/clears `RESET_REASON_REG` is commented out, so `data->reset_cause` stays 0 and `show_reset_cause` always returns `00` even though the attribute is registered. Either wire it up or drop the `reset_cause` attribute + `RESET_REASON_REG` for this PR.

5. **License mismatch.** The file header says GPLv3+ ("version 3 ... or any later version"), but `MODULE_LICENSE("GPL")` declares GPLv2 to the kernel. Please reconcile them (in-tree SONiC drivers use GPLv2).

**Description**

6. The PR body says "Added **cb_fpga** driver," but the driver added here is **`cb_pld`** (driver name, filename, and the `new_device` string). Please fix the description to match.

**switch_host_module.py**

7. `_i2c_get_reg` docstring now lists a second `bool` return ("bool: True if operation succeeded"), but the function returns a single `int` (value, or `-1`). Please drop that line.

8. `get_oper_status` only guards `== 'ERR'`, then does `int(result, 0)`. A successful-but-empty/garbage sysfs read would raise inside the function instead of returning `MODULE_STATUS_FAULT`. Wrapping the parse in a try/except → FAULT would match the old ioctl path's robustness.

**chassis.py**

9. `get_liquid_cooling()` docstring says "Lazily construct and return ... (cached)" but the body just `return None` — stale docstring, please fix (the `is_liquid_cooled() → False` pairing is correct for the air-cooled H6-128).

**sysfs.py**

10. Both helpers `print(...)` on error, but the rest of this PR moved to `sonic_py_common.logger`. Please log instead so daemon stdout stays clean. Also `fd.close()` inside `with open(...)` is redundant, and the `write_sysfs_file` docstring ("the value read will be written on reg_name") is garbled.

**init.sh**

11. `echo cb_pld 0x60 > .../i2c-14/new_device` isn't idempotent — a re-run produces a "device already exists" kernel warning. Consider guarding it (`[ -e /sys/bus/i2c/devices/14-0060 ] || echo cb_pld 0x60 > ...`), and please confirm this runs before `switch_host_module` first reads the sysfs paths.

Reviewed via interactive multi-model review — this looks correct and well-scoped; leaving as a non-blocking comment.

The fix correctly disambiguates the two very different states that both surface as `ConditionResult=no`:
- a genuinely condition-skipped unit (e.g. a disabled feature) — systemd actually evaluated the condition, so `ConditionTimestampMonotonic` is nonzero → stays non-blocking (healthy);
- a stopped static service that systemd garbage-collected/reloaded — the condition was never (re-)evaluated, so `ConditionTimestampMonotonic` is `0` → now correctly reported as Down/Inactive instead of being masked as OK.

Gating on a nonzero monotonic timestamp matches systemd semantics, and the paired tests cover both branches (nonzero ts → OK/condition-unmet; zero ts → NOT OK/Down/Inactive).

Non-blocking nit: the rationale currently lives only in the test docstring — a one-line comment at the timestamp gate in `get_unit_status` would help future readers understand why `ConditionResult=no` alone isn't sufficient. Not required for merge.

@saiarcot895 — would appreciate a second set of eyes on the systemd assumption here (that `ConditionTimestampMonotonic` stays `0` until the unit's conditions are actually evaluated), since the whole fix rests on that discriminator.

I see two issues with the PR:
- The watchdog utility will not work because there is no platform API implementation.
- Running a Python daemon on the BMC just to send keepalive messages to the watchdog seems like overkill. This can be achieved with the cron daemon, using less RAM and providing better stability.

> I see two issues with the PR:
> 
> * The watchdog utility will not work because there is no platform API implementation.

I implemented the platform api for our platform alone to show a proof of concept. If I had to do this in the aspeed platform service package then I needed to have a common base class for the Platform, Chassis and Watchdog classes. I thought its better to discuss with the community before going down that path. The other obvious way is to remove this watchdog service from aspeed platform service package completely and let each vendor implement it as per their design.

> * Running a Python daemon on the BMC just to send keepalive messages to the watchdog seems like overkill. This can be achieved with the cron daemon, using less RAM and providing better stability.

How would watchdogutil {arm [-s]/disarm/status} work if we go the cron way? If periodic petting is the *only* thing needed I could have enabled it in Systemd itself - https://www.freedesktop.org/software/systemd/man/latest/systemd-system.conf.html?__goaway_challenge=meta-refresh&__goaway_id=3e21cded61972caf3dfde0291378fa89&__goaway_referer=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.google.com%2F#RuntimeWatchdogSec=

@Xichen96 I re-reviewed this more carefully. The important distinction is that this PR is not just deduplicating `-iu`; it removes sibling `VLAN_INTERFACE` entries from `-iu` entirely. That seems correct for the normal T0/customer-VLAN model: each relay instance uses its own VLAN as downstream (`-id VlanX`), and upstream should be real L3 server-facing paths like PortChannels/native routed interfaces, not peer client VLANs. It also removes the secondary-subnet duplicate `-iu VlanX` symptom as a consequence.

One thing worth confirming: do we support any topology where a DHCP server is intentionally reachable through another local VLAN SVI? ISC `dhcrelay` treats `-iu` as the interface where server replies are accepted, so if such a topology is supported, dropping all sibling VLAN `-iu` entries could break it. If sibling VLANs are never valid server-facing paths in SONiC DHCP relay configs, then this PR looks good to me and should land before #27277 rebases.

@StormLiangMS — I went through the topo/minigraph configs to check the sibling-VLAN-server case, and no, SONiC never reaches a DHCP server via a local VLAN SVI:

- `dhcp_servers` is a **single global lab var** — `['192.0.0.1'..'192.0.0.4']` in `ansible/group_vars/lab/lab.yml` — applied uniformly via the minigraph templates (`<DhcpRelays>`). It's not set per-topo.
- `192.0.0.x` appears in **0 of the 180** `ansible/vars/topo_*.yml` files, so no topology defines the servers as a VLAN/connected subnet.
- VLAN subnets are a **different** range (`192.168.x.x/2x`); the servers' `192.0.0.0/24` is off-VLAN, reached over the routed uplinks.

So a relayed reply to the giaddr always returns over the upstream L3 path (PortChannels / routed `INTERFACE`s) — exactly what the new template lists as `-iu` — and a peer/client VLAN is never a server-facing path. Dropping sibling-VLAN `-iu` is safe.

(Scope note: this is the sonic-mgmt supported topology set — the authoritative reference for what SONiC configures.) Agreed this should land before #27277 (https://github.com/sonic-net/sonic-buildimage/pull/27277).

Gentle ping 🙏 — the topology / off-VLAN-servers question is answered inline: verified against the lab minigraph templates and all topo_*.yml that every VLAN is downstream-facing (members map to Servers endpoints, upstream neighbors are on routed ports/PortChannels), so dropping sibling Vlans from the `-iu` list is safe. CI green. @yxieca @StormLiangMS re-review appreciated. Thanks!

@Xichen96, removing all sibling `VLAN_INTERFACE` entries from `-iu` appears to regress the cross-VLAN DHCP-server support added by #2946 to resolve #1247. That issue specifically reports DHCP offers being dropped when the server is connected through another VLAN because its interface is absent from dhcrelay's listening interfaces.

The sonic-mgmt topology evidence shows that current testbeds use routed uplinks, but it does not establish that the supported same-ToR, server-on-another-VLAN configuration has been deprecated. Could we either preserve the server-facing VLAN, select upstream VLANs based on the configured server route, or provide a regression test showing that a server directly connected through another local VLAN still works after this change?

@StormLiangMS, you are right. My earlier topology argument was insufficient: https://github.com/sonic-net/sonic-buildimage/pull/2946 and https://github.com/sonic-net/sonic-buildimage/issues/1247 establish that a sibling VLAN can be a valid server-facing upstream for a relay process.

I narrowed the change to deduplication only. Each relay's own downstream VLAN is still excluded by `name != vlan_name`, while every sibling VLAN remains in `-iu` exactly once.

Before, when `Vlan1000` has primary and secondary prefixes:

```text
dhcrelay ... -id Vlan2000 -iu Vlan1000 -iu Vlan1000 -iu PortChannel01 -iu PortChannel02 -iu PortChannel03 -iu PortChannel04 ...
```

After:

```text
dhcrelay ... -id Vlan2000 -iu Vlan1000 -iu PortChannel01 -iu PortChannel02 -iu PortChannel03 -iu PortChannel04 ...
```

Non-duplicate cases remain unchanged, including configurations containing `-iu Vlan2000 -iu Vlan3000`. The same correction is applied to `dhcpmon` and both py2/py3 expected outputs.
